Unfair policies will not succeed; they put ourselves and others at risk. God gives Abimelech notice of his danger from sin, and the danger of death because of his sin. Every deliberate sinner is destined to die, but Abimelech claims he did not know. If our consciences tell us that, even if we were tricked into a trap, we have not knowingly sinned against God, it will be a source of joy on the day of trouble. It brings comfort to those who are honest that God knows their honesty and will acknowledge it. It is a great mercy to be prevented from committing sin; God must receive glory for this. But if we have done wrong unknowingly, that does not excuse us if we knowingly continue in it. Whoever does wrong, whether he is a prince or a commoner, will certainly pay for the wrong he has done unless he repents and, if possible, makes amends.

Here you can see much to criticize, even in Abraham, the father of faith. Notice his distrust of God, his excessive concern about life, and his intention to deceive. He also put temptation in the way of others, caused them affliction, exposed himself and Sarah to deserved rebukes, and yet tried to offer an excuse. These things are written for our warning, not for us to copy. Even Abraham has nothing to boast about; he cannot be declared righteous by his actions but must owe his justification to that righteousness which is upon all and given to all who believe. We should not condemn everyone as hypocrites if they fall into sin, provided they do not continue in it. But those who are unhumbled and unrepentant should take care that they do not keep on sinning, thinking that grace may be abundant. Abimelech, being warned by God, takes the warning; and truly afraid of sin and its consequences, he rose early to follow the instructions given him.

We often trouble ourselves, and are even led into temptation and sin, because of unfounded suspicions; and find the fear of God where we did not expect it. Agreements to deceive generally end in shame and sorrow; and restraints from sin, though through suffering, should be gratefully acknowledged. Though the Lord rebukes, he will pardon and deliver his people, and give them favor in the sight of those with whom they live; and control their weaknesses when they are humbled for them, so that they will prove useful to themselves and others.
